revision.text:
[[en:Cintāmaṇicakra]][[ja:如意輪観音]][[th:จินตามณีจักร]][[zh:如意輪觀音]] {{Short description|Bodhisattva and manifestation of Avalokiteśvara|qq=}} {{Infobox Buddha | name = Cintāmaṇicakra | image = Avalokitesvara Bodhisattva Siddham Script.jpeg |image caption = | sanskrit_name = चिन्तामणिचक्र
({{ill|International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration|en|International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration|qid=Q194486|lt=IAST|1=International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration}}) Cintāmaṇicakra | burmese_name = | chinese_name = (Traditional)
如意輪觀音(菩薩)
(Simplified)
如意轮观音(菩萨)
({{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Rúyìlún Guānyīn (Púsà)'') | japanese_name = {{ruby-ja|如意輪観音(菩薩)|にょいりんかんのんぼさつ|qq=}}
([[:en:Romaji|Romaji]]: ''Nyoirin Kannon (Bosatsu)'') | karen_name = | khmer_name = | korean_name = 여의륜관음(보살)
({{ill|Revised Romanization of Korean|en|Revised Romanization of Korean|qid=Q498721|lt=RR|1=Revised Romanization of Korean}}: ''Yeouiryun Gwaneum (Bosal)'') | mongolian_name = | okinawan_name = | shan_name = | thai_name = | tibetan_name = ཡིད་བཞིན་འཁོར་ལོ་
Wylie: Yid bzhin 'khor lo
THL: Yizhin Khorlo | vietnamese_name = Như Ý Luân Quán Âm Bồ Tát | sinhalese_name = | veneration = [[:en:Mahāyāna|Mahāyāna]], [[:en:Vajrayāna|Vajrayāna]] |caption=Statue of Cintamanicakra ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: ''Ruyilun Guanyin'') in the {{ill|Buddha Tooth Relic Temple and Museum|en|Buddha Tooth Relic Temple and Museum|qid=Q839965|lt=Buddha Tooth Relic Temple and Museum|1=Buddha Tooth Relic Temple and Museum}} in {{ill|Chinatown, Singapore|en|Chinatown, Singapore|qid=Q1318670|lt=Chinatown|1=Chinatown, Singapore}} in {{ill|Singapore|en|Singapore|qid=Q334|lt=Singapore|1=Singapore}}|qq=}} '''Cintāmaṇicakra''' ({{langx|sa|चिन्तामणिचक्र|qq=}}; {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}} ({{ill|Traditional Chinese characters|en|Traditional Chinese characters|qid=Q178528|lt=Traditional|1=Traditional Chinese characters}}): 如意輪觀音; {{ill|Simplified Chinese characters|en|Simplified Chinese characters|qid=Q185614|lt=Simplified|1=Simplified Chinese characters}}: 如意轮观音; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Rúyìlún Guānyīn''; {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}}: 如意輪観音, ''Nyoirin Kannon'') is a {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} and a manifestation of {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}} (known in Chinese as [[Kannon|Guanyin]]). He is counted as one of {{ill|Six Guanyin|en|Six Guanyin|qid=Q60989433|lt=six different forms|1=Six Guanyin}} of the bodhisattva that represent salvation afforded to beings among the six realms of {{ill|Saṃsāra (Buddhism)|en|Saṃsāra (Buddhism)|qid=Q10993736|lt=saṃsāra|1=Saṃsāra (Buddhism)}}. Among these incarnations, Cintāmaṇicakra is believed to save those in the {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=deva|1=Deva (Buddhism)}} realm.{{cite web |title=六観音 七観音 八大観音 |url=http://tobifudo.jp/butuzo/7kanindex.html |website=Flying Deity Tobifudō (Ryūkō-zan Shōbō-in Official Website) |access-date=2021-11-21|qq=}} Cintāmaṇicakra is sometimes also referred to as '''Avalokiteśvara as Mahābrahmā the Profound''' (大梵深遠觀音; Ch. ''Dàfàn Shēnyuǎn Guānyīn''; Jp. ''Daibon Jin'on Kannon''). ==Iconography== [[File:Cintamanicakra (Ruyilun Guanyin) - Jade Buddha Temple; Shanghai, China.jpg|thumb|270px|left|Six-armed Cintāmaṇicakra in the Hall of Great Compassion in [[Jade Buddha Temple]], {{ill|Shanghai|en|Shanghai|qid=Q8686|lt=Shanghai, China|1=Shanghai}}]] Cintāmaṇicakra is depicted as having anywhere from two to sixteen arms, with the two-armed and six-armed forms being the more common in Chinese and Japanese art. In his six-armed form, Cintāmaṇicakra is commonly shown wearing a crown with an effigy of [[:en:Amitābha Buddha|Amitābha Buddha]] and sitting in a "royal" position (''{{ill|Lalitasana|en|Lalitasana|qid=Q75332455|lt=mahārājalīlāsana|1=Lalitasana}}'', i.e. with his left leg tucked inwards and his right knee raised) atop a lotus on a rock protruding from the ocean - a symbol of {{ill|Mount Potalaka|en|Mount Potalaka|qid=Q11626848|lt=Mount Potalaka|1=Mount Potalaka}}, Avalokiteśvara's legendary abode.{{cite web |title=A Late Tang Dynasty Sculpture Bought at a Missouri Garage Sale for Less Than $100 Just Sold for $2.1 Million |url=https://news.artnet.com/market/chinese-buddhist-sculpture-garage-sale-1495570 |website=artnet |accessdate=2021-04-02 |date=2019|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Chutiwongs |first1=Nandana |editor1-last=Klokke |editor1-first=Marijke J. |editor2-last=Lunsingh Scheurleer |editor2-first=Pauline |title=Ancient Indonesian Sculpture |date=1994 |publisher=KITLV Press |pages=102–103 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=cXSsPPZepXwC&pg=PA103 |chapter=An aspect of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara in Ancient Indonesia |isbn=9789067180764 |qq=}}{{Dead link|date=December 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes |qq=}} His first right hand touches his face in a {{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|qid=Q208030|lt=pensive mudra|1=Mudra}}, his second right hand holds a ''[[Cintamani|cintāmaṇi]]'', and his third right hand holds {{ill|Japamala|en|Japamala|qid=Q1336519|lt=prayer beads|1=Japamala}}. His first left hand meanwhile touches the rock base he is sitting on, his second left hand holds a [[:en:Padma (attribute)|crimson lotus flower]] (''padma''), and the third left hand holds a {{ill|Dharmachakra|en|Dharmachakra|qid=Q665056|lt=Dharma wheel|1=Dharmachakra}} (''cakra'').{{cite book |last1=Epprecht |first1=Katharina |title=Kannon: Divine Compassion: Early Buddhist Art from Japan |date=2007 |publisher=Museum Rietberg |isbn=978-3-9070-7729-0 |page=37|qq=}} In two-armed images, he does not hold a jewel and he may be seated with his right leg crossed at the ankle over his left leg. This imagery is similar to that of the statue of [[Maitreya]] at {{ill|Chūgū-ji|en|Chūgū-ji|qid=Q1091232|lt=Chūgū-ji|1=Chūgū-ji}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara|1=Nara Prefecture}}, which has been mistakenly venerated as Cintāmaṇicakra.{{cite web |title=Nyoirin Kannon 如意輪観音 |url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nyoirinkannon.htm |website=JAANUS |accessdate=2019-02-26 |date=2001|qq=}}{{cite web |title=本尊 国宝 菩薩半跏像（伝如意輪観音） |url=http://www.chuguji.jp/archaic-smile/ |website=Shōtoku-shū Chūgū-ji Official Website |access-date=2021-11-21|qq=}} Another two-armed form exists where he holds a ''cintāmaṇi'' in his right hand and a water vase in his left hand. An example of this variation is the colossal [[Kannon|Guanyin]] statue located in {{ill|Tsz Shan Monastery|en|Tsz Shan Monastery|qid=Q7850444|lt=Tsz Shan Monastery|1=Tsz Shan Monastery}} in {{ill|Hong Kong|en|Hong Kong|qid=Q8646|lt=Hong Kong|1=Hong Kong}}. Cintāmaṇicakra may also be abstractly represented via his attributes or symbols (''{{ill|Samaya|en|Samaya|qid=Q7408931|lt=Samaya|1=Samaya}}''; Ch. 三昧耶形, ''sānmèiyé xíng''; Jp. ''sa(n)maya-gyō''), the ''cintāmaṇi'' and the lotus flower. == Dhāraṇī and mantras == === Cintāmaṇicakra Dhāraṇī === Several mantras are associated with Cintāmaṇicakra. In Chinese Buddhism, the '''Cintāmaṇicakra Dhāraṇī''' or '''Cintāmaṇi [[Chakravarti (Sanskrit term)|Cakravarti]] Dhāraṇī''' (如意寶輪王陀羅尼; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Rúyì Bǎolún Wáng Tuóluóní'') is reckoned as one of the Ten Small Mantras (十小咒; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Shí xiǎo zhòu''), a collection of {{ill|Mantra|en|Mantra|qid=Q131510|lt=Mantra|1=Mantra}}s and [[:en:Dhāraṇī|Dhāraṇī]]s commonly recited in temples during morning liturgical services.{{Cite web|title=Ten Small Mantras|url=http://www.buddhamountain.ca/Ten_Small_Mantras.php|access-date=2021-04-26|website=www.buddhamountain.ca|qq=}}{{Cite web|title=What is Ten Small Mantras|url=https://www.buddhismtoronto.com/mantra-3.1.php|access-date=2021-04-26|website=www.buddhismtoronto.com|qq=}} The dhāraṇī originates from the ''Cintāmaṇicakra Dhāraṇī Sutra'', which was translated into Chinese by the monks {{ill|Bodhiruci|en|Bodhiruci|qid=Q2324914|lt=Bodhiruci|1=Bodhiruci}} (如意輪陀羅尼經; pinyin: ''Rúyìlún tuóluóní jīng''; {{ill|Taishō Tripiṭaka|en|Taishō Tripiṭaka|qid=Q837315|lt=Taishō Tripiṭaka|1=Taishō Tripiṭaka}} 1080{{cite web |title=如意輪陀羅尼經 第1卷 |url=http://tripitaka.cbeta.org/T20n1080_001 |website=CBETA Chinese Electronic Tripiṭaka Collection (漢文大藏經) |access-date=2021-11-21|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yijing (monk)|en|Yijing (monk)|qid=Q457629|lt=Yijing|1=Yijing (monk)}} (佛說觀自在菩薩如意心陀羅尼咒經; pinyin: ''Fóshuō Guānzìzài Púsà rúyì xīn tuóluóní zhòu jīng''; T. 1081{{cite web |title=佛說觀自在菩薩如意心陀羅尼呪經 |url=http://tripitaka.cbeta.org/T20n1081_001 |website=CBETA Chinese Electronic Tripiṭaka Collection (漢文大藏經) |access-date=2021-11-21|qq=}}). In this sutra, Avalokitesvara states that this dharani "has great power and majesty, like the celestial wish-fulfilling tree, raining down great treasures upon the wise. Whatever is desired, all can be obtained, like the jewel of Maṇi. It can fulfill all the superior aspirations of all sentient beings." The dhāraṇī is: {| class="wikitable" |+ !Bodhiruci's Sanskrit ({{ill|International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration|en|International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration|qid=Q194486|lt=IAST|1=International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration}}) !Chinese translation (Yijing) !Pinyin |- |''Namo {{ill|Three Jewels and Three Roots|en|Three Jewels and Three Roots|qid=Q7797776|lt=ratna-trayāya|1=Three Jewels and Three Roots}}{{efn|Yijing's text instead has: ''Namo {{ill|Buddhahood|en|Buddhahood|qid=Q56247062|lt=buddhāya|1=Buddhahood}}, namo {{ill|Dharma (Buddhism)|en|Dharma (Buddhism)|qid=Q9241518|lt=dharmāya|1=Dharma (Buddhism)}}, namaḥ {{ill|Sangha|en|Sangha|qid=Q193944|lt=saṃghāya|1=Sangha}}''.|qq=}}
Nama Āryāvalokiteśvarāya bodhisattvāya mahāsattvāya mahākāruṇikāya
tadyathā
Oṃ cakravarti cintāmaṇi mahāpadme ru ru tiṣṭhat jvala ākarṣāya hūṃ phaṭ [[:en:Svāhā|Svāhā]]
Oṃ padma cintāmaṇi jvala hūṃ
Oṃ varada padme hūṃ'' |南謨佛馱耶　南謨達摩耶　南謨僧伽耶
南謨觀自在菩薩摩訶薩　具大悲心者
怛姪他
唵斫羯羅伐底　震多末尼謨訶　鉢蹬謎　嚕嚕嚕嚕　底瑟他　篅攞痾羯利沙也　吽發莎訶
菴鉢踏摩　震多末尼　篅攞吽
菴跋剌陀　鉢亶謎吽 |''Nāmó fótuóyé nāmó dámóyé nāmó sēngjiāyé
Nāmó Guānzìzài púsà móhēsà jù dàbēixīnzhě
dázhítā
Ān zhuójiéluófádǐ zhènduōmòní móhē bōdēngmí lǔ lǔ lǔ lǔ dǐsètā shuòlà ējiélìshāyě hōng fā suōhē
Ān bōtàmó zhènduōmòní shuòlà hōng
Ān bálátuó bōdǎnmí hōng'' |} The Buddha then praises the dhāraṇī spell in verse as follows:
Excellent, excellent, good man, You are able to compassionately consider all sentient beings, By expounding this wish-fulfilling dhāraṇī, You deliver sentient beings, bringing them great superior benefits. For those who believe and accept it, their sins will be eradicated, And they will transcend the three realms and realize bodhi (enlightenment). Wherever there are practitioners of this dhāraṇī, Both worldly and transcendental wishes will be completely fulfilled.
Then Avalokitesvara offers some basic instructions on how to practice the dharani:
World-Honored One, if there are bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, upāsikās, boys, or girls who seek great merit and blessings in this life, they should, day and night, diligently practice in accordance with the law and uphold this Cintāmaṇi-cakra Dhāraṇī mantra. There is no need to select auspicious days, months, or constellations, nor is it necessary to fast for one or two days, nor to bathe or establish an altar. They should wear their usual clothes, purify themselves with clear water, and conduct the ritual as usual. During day and night, they should reside in a clean room, sit facing east in the lotus posture, visualize the perfect form of Avalokiteśvara, whose body radiates great light like the rising sun and who is seated on a lotus flower. They should focus their mind on reciting without distraction, burn agarwood incense, and offer it with sincerity. They should reverently worship and make offerings of incense and flowers as they wish, ensuring these offerings are continuous. They should recite the dhāraṇī 1,080 times during each of the six periods of the day, without interruption. After completing 300,000 recitations, through the yogic practice of visualization and mantra recitation, all the heinous crimes committed in the past and present, such as the five uninterrupted sins and extremely severe karmic obstacles, will naturally be eradicated. They will have auspicious dreams, which will signify the elimination of their karmic sins. The sacred Avalokiteśvara will bless and protect them, and through this virtuous root, they will accomplish hundreds of thousands of tasks. By merely raising their mind and reciting, all their endeavors will be fully accomplished. There is no other divine power or mantra that can match the miraculous power of this Cintāmaṇi-cakra Dhāraṇī. Why is this? If someone is able to believe in and uphold this dhāraṇī, then all the karmic obstacles accumulated from committing the four major offenses, the five rebellious sins, and the ten evil acts, which would cause one to fall into the Avīci Hell, will all be eradicated....By maintaining this practice consistently without transgression, they will see the sacred Avalokiteśvara appear before them in a golden body, removing all defilements and karmic obstacles, and granting blessings with divine power.
=== Other mantras === The ''[[:en:Bījā|bījāmantra]]'' or seed syllable mantra used to symbolically represent Cintāmaṇicakra is '''{{IAST|hrīḥ|qq=}}''' ({{ill|Siddhaṃ script|en|Siddhaṃ script|qid=Q250379|lt=Siddhaṃ|1=Siddhaṃ script}}: [[File:.Bonji-hrīḥ.svg|20px|frameless]]; {{ill|Devanagari|en|Devanagari|qid=Q38592|lt=Devanagari|1=Devanagari}}: ह्रीः), which is the bija of the Lotus {{ill|Five Tathāgatas|en|Five Tathāgatas|qid=Q265911|lt=Buddha family|1=Five Tathāgatas}}. The two shorter mantras found in the dharani are also commonly employed in the Japanese tradition on their own: * ''Oṃ padme cintāmaṇi jvala hūṃ'' (Japanese pronunciation: ''On handomei shindamani jinbara un''){{cite book |last1=Ōguri |first1=Dōei |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KVoclyXzHZYC&pg=PT11 |title=図説般若心経入門 (Zusetsu Hannya Shingyō nyūmon) |date=2002 |publisher=Suzuki Shuppan |isbn=978-4-7902-1105-1|qq=}} * ''Oṃ varada padme hūṃ (On barada handomei un){{cite book |last1=Ōmori |first1=Gijō |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NcjB_-rTzIUC&pg=PA58 |title=実修真言宗の密教と修行 (Jisshū Shingon-shū no mikkyō to shūgyō) |date=2010 |publisher=Gakken Publishing |isbn=978-4-0540-4524-8 |page=58|qq=}}'' {| class="wikitable" |+ |} == Gallery == File:Cintamanicakra (Ruyilun Guanyin) - Mount Putuo Guanyin Dharma Realm; Zhejiang, China.jpg|Six-armed Cintāmaṇicakra in {{ill|Mount Putuo|en|Mount Putuo|qid=Q716232|lt=Mount Putuo|1=Mount Putuo}} Guanyin Dharma Realm, {{ill|Zhejiang|en|Zhejiang|qid=Q16967|lt=Zhejiang|1=Zhejiang}}, China File:慈 山 寺,香港 -Tsz Shan Monastery, Hong Kong - 33986713598.jpg|76 meter tall statue of the two-armed Cintāmaṇicakra in {{ill|Tsz Shan Monastery|en|Tsz Shan Monastery|qid=Q7850444|lt=Tsz Shan Monastery|1=Tsz Shan Monastery}} in {{ill|Hong Kong|en|Hong Kong|qid=Q8646|lt=Hong Kong|1=Hong Kong}} File:No. 2 - Picture of Cintamanicakra (如意輪觀音 or 如意轮观音; Ruyilun Guanyin) in a Chinese Buddhist tract on the Nilakantha Dharani, or Great Compassion Mantra (大悲咒; Dàbēi zhòu), corresponding to line 2.png|Picture of Cintāmaṇicakra in a Chinese Buddhist tract on the {{ill|Nīlakaṇṭha Dhāraṇī|en|Nīlakaṇṭha Dhāraṇī|qid=Q2310317|lt=Nīlakaṇṭha Dhāraṇī|1=Nīlakaṇṭha Dhāraṇī}} File:Nyoirin-kannon (11140621175).jpg|Two-armed Cintāmaṇicakra in [[Tōdai-ji]], {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} File:Bodhisattva Cintāmaṇicakra, Kamakura period, Japan.jpg|Cintāmaṇicakra, 1275, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}, Japan File:如意輪観音 - Twelve-Armed Cintamanicakra (Nyoirin Kannon).jpg|Twelve-armed Cintāmaṇicakra flanked by the [[Wisdom King]] {{ill|Kuṇḍali|en|Kuṇḍali|qid=Q1064734|lt=Kuṇḍali|1=Kuṇḍali}} and the bodhisattva Vajragarbha ==See also== * [[Cintamani]] * [[Om mani padme hum]] * {{ill|Ryōgen|en|Ryōgen|qid=Q543930|lt=Ryōgen|1=Ryōgen}} - Japanese {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} monk popularly believed to be the incarnation of Cintāmaṇicakra ==Notes== {{notelist|qq=}} ==References== {{reflist|2|qq=}} {{Avalokiteśvara|qq=}} {{Bodhisattvas|qq=}} {{Chinese Buddhist Pantheon|qq=}} {{Buddhism topics|qq=}} [[en:Cintāmaṇicakra]] [[en:Cintāmaṇicakra]] {{Commons category|Cintāmaṇicakra}} [[ja:如意輪観音]] [[th:จินตามณีจักร]] [[zh:如意輪觀音]] {{wikidata link|Q11446712}} [[Category:Bodhisattvas]] [[Category:Avalokiteśvara]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]]
